speaker: interviewer
text: Four. Do you know of any instances where children who were hidden who were reunited with their families after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First of all, I have to tell you that we had contact with an organization, a student organization, in Amsterdam who picked children out of the theater where they were brought together separate from the parents. And he-- when he walked around in the morning, they picked a couple of kids out of there. And-- and so the guards couldn't see that. And [? I think ?] [? they had ?] seven or eight kids they brought them to Lindberg, too. And I picked them up and brought them to [? John ?] [? Bosch. ?] And he looked for hiding places for those kids. And after the war, of course, you were registered by the Red Cross, all these kids. And they were united with the parents. A little girl we had at home, the parents knocked at our door once, and they came to pick up little Rachel. And [INAUDIBLE] my parents were more or less disappointed. And the reason why they were [INAUDIBLE] that's the little girl. They were very attached to the little girl. So it was heartbreaking when have to-- when they have to say goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those parents were very understandable. And they said, well, listen she shared for a year. Our parents died in camp. And we have no relatives anymore. Can we adopt you as grandparents? And that's what they did. So after that, my mom and my father visited her and the people in Amsterdam where Rachel was. And the first big holiday, Rachel was allowed to stay at our place, you know. [? And there was a ?] fantastic, good relationship with that. And [? we ?] would call them grandpa and grandma. It was like [? being ?] in our family. How long was that little girl with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end of the war-- till September 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was she at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two or three years, I believe-- something like that, yeah, two or three years, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it was like for her to leave your parents and go to her biological parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was crying, of course, in the beginning. But we comfort her and say, come back. And you know, Grandma will be here. So that way we-- there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you maintained contact with this little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the little girl was later on immigrated with the family to Israel. And there she married and served there [? in the ?] Israeli army. And she was involved in a car accident, was killed in a car accident, yeah. So I met her [INAUDIBLE] daughter when I was in Israel, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this accident happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, short-- shortly after when they immigrated. [? I thought ?] maybe in beginning of 1950, something like that. She was 18 or 19 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've met her daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when I was in Israel. I met her daughter, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of any instances where children were not reunited with their parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There are lots of children, of course, who had no parents anymore. They were murdered in the Auschwitz or whatever. But one guy, for example, he stood by the name of [? Lody ?] [? Weinbergen. ?] He was in concentration camp with his parents in the northern part of Holland. And he got very seriously sick. So he had to go to a hospital in the city of Groningen. And his parents, they smuggled in [INAUDIBLE] to one of the [INAUDIBLE] a chance to-- to steal him out of the hospital. They give permission for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to this hospital. And had a nurse-- we found a nurse we knew already who had opportunity to take him out of the hospital in Groningen. And my sister later on picked him up, and we found a hiding place for him in Maastricht, neighborhood [? from ?] Maastricht.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, of course, his parents didn't come back. But they found it better that he was brought up in a Jewish community. So they brought him to a Jewish orphan home in Amsterdam. Then he grew up, and then he immigrated to Israel. And his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adopted parents were very [INAUDIBLE] angry about that, because he was attached to the boy. But he came back later and did everything for them, you know. He paid to put them to Israel, and he visited them several times. And when those people died a couple of years ago, I believe they had no children. Their only child was that Jewish boy. In the will, the house was for him. He got that house there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they formed rather strong attachments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very attached. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he when he was in the hospital and he was sort of rescued?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was around 5-years-old. 4, 5-years-old. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was he with his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end of-- till the end of the end of the war. Till the beginning-- the second half of 1945, when they took him from the [INAUDIBLE] to an orphan home, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of any cases where there was difficulty between the parents whose children were hidden and the people who hid the child? Did some of the people not want to give up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This particular boy-- there were people who didn't want to give him up, in particular, because his parents didn't come back. And it became a court case. And they just ordered him to go back to the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been difficult for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult for them, yeah. People that had no children, of course they were attached to him. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did some of the Jewish parents who came to get their children who were hidden but were raised as Christians-- how did the Jewish parents react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably most of them, they understood the circumstances, because, you know, if you want to live a normal life, you have to act normal. But there was never the problem, no. I mean, you cannot make a person Christian by saying they're Christian. I mean, it had to come in your heart. [INAUDIBLE] So it didn't make any sense. It's something that came from within.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. So you can deny. You can be baptized. You can be Christian. [? But ?] [? does that ?] make [? you ?] Christian? After the war, were you involved in accusing anyone of war crimes or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was-- when I came back out of concentration camp, I was approached by the local police force. And they asked me if I was willing to join them, especially to investigate political prisoners. And I admit to that. So I became part of the force. I interrogated Nazi people, but also Germans who were-- were Gestapo agents and so on. And one of them was Richard Nitch who interrogated me in-- when I was arrested. He was one of the guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the one who was hitting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to go, and he shot my friend, Drik Van Assen. And he had to go with this to point where he was buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did that? He showed you where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He had no other way, because he was a prisoners, you know. So we had him in a car. And when the people in that part of Lindberg where most farmers live heard who he was, they came with their tools running to the car. They want to hit him. We had to protect him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything else about that particular trip to find your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a very hot day. And everybody [? on turn ?] to go to into a restaurant to have a cool drink. And I went in the restaurant for a cool drink, and he came out with another one for him. And he give him that, and he said-- he looked at me. And he bent his hat. I said, what you do that for? He said, I'm a Christian. I want to show you that we don't hate. And well, I guess there he was more [INAUDIBLE] than anything else that we expected there. But he was later on-- he kept fighting a 20 years sentence. And a couple of years ago, he was released and sent back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long was he in prison roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, roughly maybe 20, 30 years at least here. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in jail that long?

speaker: interviewee
text: In jail that long, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he imprisoned?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the city, Breda-- B-R-E-D-A. In the southern part of Holland there was a big prison, and he was-- he was there in that prison, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any other trials after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The people who were-- were the traitor, [? Vosenhaut. ?] He was also in court, and I was a witness there, too, to give testimony to what he did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in his case?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also convicted to, I guess, 10 or 15 years in prison, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he serve that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time he did. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after that, he was released?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was released, yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I lost track of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever threatened, or did anyone retaliate against you for having testified?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one bothered you around that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: A different line of questioning-- how has your life been since the war? What's your life been like since that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left home. I make it into Canada, because it was dangerous there. They had a lot of people [? out of ?] the underground. And the only thing they spoke about if you met them was what happened in the past. And I'm not the type that's always looked to the past. I like to look in the future. So I got sick and tired of that. And I decided with my wife to immigrate to Canada. I start a new life here, and that we did. And we ended up in Ottawa, and I'm still living here. We arrived here in 1958.

speaker: interviewer
text: What prompted you to choose Canada of all the countries in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people mentioned South Africa, but I was against the apartheid, so we didn't go there. My brother lived in Australia, and I found it too far away to visit my parents once in a while. So I thought the closest way was Canada. And if you need [? help, ?] you had no problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your decision was partly influenced by the fact that it was the Canadians who were very present during the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily because the southern part from Holland was liberated with the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, so I mean, they-- they didn't know anything about the Canadians. That was only in the northern part, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has your experience during the war time and the things that you did to rescue the Jews-- how has that affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. I used to [INAUDIBLE] before. You know, I started my own business here in Ottawa. I am an insurance broker. The only thing that I find that I have an obligation now to tell other people, especially younger generation, what happened during war time as a witness. And I like to, you know, especially with those people [INAUDIBLE] coming up about there was no Holocaust, I can tell them, [? the kids ?] [? that's ?] the biggest lie ever I have heard. [? So that ?] [? there is ?] the truth. And I get an opportunity to go to schools, and I'm registered in the Jewish community here to-- if they ask for a resource, then they call me sometimes, ask me to go to a school and talk to the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there specific reasons why you endangered yourself and your family to hide those Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The particular reason was, of course, our commitment as a Christian to help those people in need. And at that particular time, [? we ?] [? wanted the ?] Jewish. And for that reason, we help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you chose not to turn your back and look the other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you glad that you did what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am glad what I did, but I always feel a little bit sorry that I couldn't do more. Especially, if you read all the figures [? on ?] [? all those ?] people who were murdered and never came back [? in that era. ?] And we had cases that, for example, we [? worked ?] with a teacher from the high school. He approached me, and he had a Jewish family-- father, mother, and four kids. And they were desperate to find a hiding place, because they expected to be picked up the same day. I found some people willing to put them in their homes. So I was visiting people with him. And they were putting all the clothes together. And when we were there sitting, the bell rings, and it was the Dutch police force. They are-- and on behalf of the Germans picked them up. [INAUDIBLE] we have to pick them up. And we asked the police man if he was willing to go away for half an hour. He said, I'm sorry, but I have to do my duty. And those four, those six people-- man, wife, and four kids-- they all came, and they all died in camp-- in concentration camp, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] If a similar situation were to arise, and some other group of people were persecuted, would you respond in the same way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, you never plan to do those things, you know. They-- they come-- instantly come up. And I would think I would do the same thing, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you consider yourself heroic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Here's a person who did his ordinary duties. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ordinary duties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people who turned their backs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Quite a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What differentiates you from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had not the conviction that [? we ?] [? are ?] [? here, ?] and that we're here to help each other. And they always thought about themselves. Didn't, you know-- they were afraid. And that might happen. That might happen. And if all the people would have helped the Jewish, there was no Jew who would have ended up in camp. That's my conviction, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those who turned their back did so out of fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of fear. Yeah. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a need to preserve themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I must admit, of course, that I was a bachelor at the time. I didn't have a family. Maybe if you have a wife and kids you would maybe do it in a different ways. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents had children, and they acted--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All of them were grown up, of course. And my sister was at home, and she helped fully with that. And my parents were fully involved in the undergrounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have you been recognized by some organizations for the work that you've done? Yes. I was recognized for Yad Vashem. What is Yad Vashem?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the place in Israel where they remember the people who are murdered in the Holocaust. And I was there on two times. And they have a tree planted there in the Yad Vashem. And I got a medal from the Israeli government. I got also a medal from the Dutch government. And I got also an honorary doctorate degree from the university-- Lawrence University in Canton, New York. That's the way they recognized me and all the people who were involved, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's recognition from different organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's right. And then I was recognized some years ago at a Jewish community in Ottawa. And they had an evening in a synagogue in the West End. And they recognized me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that? What was involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: They present-- it was a large gathering. There was about 700, 800 people there. And they [INAUDIBLE] from the speakers. And I got-- they had-- going to open up a Holocaust resource center, and it's named after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this resource center?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ottawa in one of the schools here, in one of the buildings here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one of the schools. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Mendel Good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mendel Good is a Jewish fellow, a wealthy gentleman, who came out of concentration camp. And he goes with me to the schools, you know. And he tells his story from his side, and I tell the story from the Christian side, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you-- would you encourage your children or your grandchildren to emulate what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I believe it is-- if you do this thing that makes you happy, if you give other people and forget about yourself, that makes you more happy than the other way around, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would encourage them to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned earlier that your children now know what you did. What about your grandchildren? Do they know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I gave them all-- all my kids, grandchildren, I gave them a copy of my book. And as soon as they are able to read it, then the parents will give them the book, so they can read it. [INAUDIBLE] parent [? can validate, ?] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your life, who is the one person who had the most influence on you? Who one individual had the most influence?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Mr. Dirk Van Assen. And he audited in the income tax department. And he was also an elder in our church, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn from Dirk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Self-sacrifice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you elaborate please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to-- to look at the need for others and don't look at [? your ?] need [? first, you know. ?] And help people who are in need.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a last question, what sort of message would you like to leave to future generations who will be observing this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message I have, and I tell also the children in school, is that the Holocaust was not only took place in the gas chambers, but it started in the heart of the people when they started to hate people for reasons of faith, or religion, or race. And if you want to avoid the next Holocaust, we should fight our own self and love the people, even if they're Jewish, or colored people, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been an honor to have been here with you this morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Van Mansum, would you please introduce this member of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sure. I would like to introduce to you my wife, Annette Van Mansum. I met her some years ago in Toronto. She's my second wife, and I'm her second husband. And I was a widower, and she was a widow. And we married in Toronto 21 years ago. And she moved from Chicago to Ottawa, and now we live together in Ottawa. And we're married for 21 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Mr. Van Mansum, would you please describe the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo is made during when I was honored by the Jewish community in Masstricht in an upper room in the synagogue, in the local synagogue. And [? there ?] was given to me a painting done by a famous artist, [? Charles Icke ?] and listed with all the names of people I helped during the German wartime, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the end of 1946. I believe it was around November 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you identify-- in addition to yourself, can you identify any of the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Mr. [? Tugentauf ?] standing there. There was the rabbi of Maastricht was standing there. And Mr. [? Slyon ?] who was later on immigrated to Israel, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Thank you. Mr. Van Mansum, would you please describe the people in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To the right is my father and besides her is my mother. And standing beside my mother and being sat by me is Fritz Freilich, who was hidden at the place till the end of the war there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in the November 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maastricht.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in any particular location in Maastricht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's also in the synagogue when they had me there, gave me the painting during the celebration with the Jewish community there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Van Mansum, who is this individual?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Fritz Freilich. He-- the picture was taken after the war, in 1945. He was hidden at our place during the wartime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Van Mansum, please tell me about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is also made during the synagogue in Maastricht in the upper room there in November, 1945. And there was a girl, the daughter of Mr. And Mrs. Wesley, [? Lanie ?] Wesley, who handed me over some flowers. And beside-- standing her mother and her father, Mr. And Mrs. Wesley and also Mr. [? Frank ?]. He helped [? me ?] in wartime. And this police officer from Maastricht who also was sometimes involved in helping me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this little girl one of the Jews that you helped rescue?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right and, she's now living under the name of Mrs. Forgas. And she lives in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a photo of yourself. Would you please describe what the photo is on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The photo was made in 1941. We were required by the Germans to carry ID cards. And that is it on one side of the ID card with a stamp on the city of Ottawa on top of it. The city of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ottawa. Sorry, the city of Maastricht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maastricht. OK. And this is the genuine item?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the genuine item. That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. And this is the flip side?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the flip side of this ID card. We made two fingerprints. One on the back of the picture and the other one separate. And with my address, and every time you changed your address, you had to go to City Hall and change it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once again, this is genuine?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is genuine. Yes. Right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. This is another identity card. Could you tell me about it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a fake identity card. It is [INAUDIBLE] on somebody who lives in Belgium. And I went-- the police officer had an artist in the police office in Maastricht. He took off his picture, put my picture on that, and copied the stamp, hand-copied the stamp. And I used that card when I traveled to Belgium once in a while to [? help ?] also people hiding in Brussels or had to bring over somebody to Belgium. I used this card. I used ID card.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a forgery.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a forgery, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please describe, Mr. Van Mansum, what we see on the screen now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. These are the food coupons we had to obtain every Monday, [? a sheet. ?] And then the papers announce what kind of number it was in that particular week. We had to use for buying either meat, or milk, or bread, until all the numbers were gone, and they had to pick up a new sheets with numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please describe what's on the screen now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a release form I got from the Germans when I was released from concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the one in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945. It must be the second one, I suppose, yeah. Yeah, that was the second one when I was arrested for the second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the concentration camp Amersfoort.

speaker: interviewee
text: Concentration camp Amersfoort. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What is the artifact that we see on the screen now?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a copy of it illegal magazine, Free Netherlands, that I was the provincial representative for this organization. I spread the magazine around.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the ones you distributed?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. What is it that's on the screen at this moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the screen at the moment is the diploma of honor that my sister Margeretha received when Yad Vashem, because she so helped me in hiding Jewish and looking after them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she received this in Jerusalem?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she received this. And that's correct, yes, in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, I believe, it was 1962. Before that she already had tree planted. And it was one of the first trees [? she ?] [? get ?] Yad Vashem is for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the first trees was from your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Thank you. What is on the screen now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is what my sister received in Yad Vashem when she planted the tree. I believe it was done in 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she one of the first to have a tree planted?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was one of the first, yeah. Her tree is one of the biggest ones. It's standing in front of the Lane of the Righteous Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Van Mansum, would you please describe this medal?

speaker: interviewee
text: The medal I received in Yad Vashem-- that meant I was honored there. I plant them a tree, and I got also this medal.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that there is a design on the back side of it and we'll flip that over to take a look at it as well. This is the reverse side of the medal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me anything about the design? Is there something specific about the design that you can share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's a well-known [INAUDIBLE] who developed. I know the saying. Well, one person helped the whole world-- something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Mr. Van Mansum, please describe this certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This certificate I got at the Israeli embassy in Ottawa in November, 1969, also given out by Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you describe this certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This certificate I received from the Jewish community in Ottawa in 1991 in January when they honored me in one of the synagogues in Ottawa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please describe this certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I received this certificate in 1945 in November, when I was honored by the Jewish community in Maastricht. And that meant the names of the people I was able to help during the wartime and who came alive out of the wartime. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the names of all the people that you rescued?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are there-- not all, a few of them are missing, but the majority of the people I helped during the wartime.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Thank you very much. This is a portrait of yourself. Would you tell me a little bit more about it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this portrait was made by a well-known-- Dutch painter, Charles [DUTCH] And that was handed to me when I was celebrated and honored by the Jewish community in Maastricht in November of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who are the people in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's my daughter, Margaret, my youngest daughter, with her husband, Rich, and their three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken last year in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Canada. OK. And those are your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? now ?] my grandchildren are living in Canada, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me who these people are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's my only son, Gerrit with his wife, Hetje and the two children, who are living now in Dallas, Texas, in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the picture taken in Dallas?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the picture was taken just before they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year, yeah. In '96, in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who are the people of this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest daughter, Ria,s and her husband, Ralph, and their four children. They are all living in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In last year, 1995, in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
